segments.s443-g17: بدو is the prepositional indirect-object 'to him'. گفت is the past tense verb 'he said' (3rd-person past of گفتن) that introduces direct speech. کار من اندر گذشت is a nominal clause meaning 'my affair/business has passed (is over)', with اندر گذشت (literally 'within passed') functioning as a poetic way to say 'is finished.' Word order is Subject/possession (کار من) + circumstantial (اندر) + verb (گذشت).
segments.s443-g18: هم here functions as an emphasiser 'even' (stressing what follows). از marks origin or source 'from'. تارکم is a possessive form (تارک + -م 'my') naming a container/thing belonging to the speaker; the exact lexical sense is archaic/poetic. آب برتر گذشت uses گذشت (past 'passed') with برتر in a spatial/relative sense 'beyond/over' — together 'the water passed over/beyond (it)'.
segments.s443-g19: نشستم is a past-tense verb 'I sat' used idiomatically here as 'I reigned' or 'I occupied kingship'. به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for kingship' (i.e. 'on the throne'). صد و بیست سال is the duration 'one hundred and twenty years' (و is the connective 'and' within the numeral).
segments.s443-g20: ندیدم is the negative past 'I did not see'. به گیتی 'in the world' (به + گیتی). کسی را uses the object marker را after 'someone' to make the person definite/accusative. همال here functions as a predicative noun meaning 'peer/equal' (the provided sense list lacks this meaning); the whole clause expresses 'I have seen no one in the world equal to him.'
segments.s443-g21: تو اکنون 'you now' sets the addressee and time. همی is an archaic/poetic present-continuous or durative marker often reinforcing the imperative/habit ('do keep ...'). کوش is an imperative 'strive/endeavor'. و combines clauses; با داد باش is an imperative phrase 'be with justice' → 'be just/fair' where داد is 'justice' and باش is the 2nd-person imperative 'be'.
segments.s443-g22: چو here means 'when' (temporal conditional). داد آوری is a 2nd-person singular verb form 'you bring' or 'you do/perform justice' (آوری from آوردن). از غم آزاد باش is an imperative 'be free from sorrow' where از marks separation ('from sorrow') and آزاد is the predicate adjective 'free', followed by the imperative باش 'be.'
segments.s443-g23: خردمند را uses را as the definite/object marker 'the wise one' as direct object. شاد و نزدیک دار is an imperative construction where شاد ('happy') and نزدیک ('near') are predicate adjectives/advectives governed by the imperative verb دار ('keep'/'hold'), i.e. 'keep the wise happy and near.' Word order places the object before its predicates and the imperative verb at the end.
segments.s443-g24: جهان بر بداندیش is a prepositional phrase 'let the world be against/for the ill‑willed' where بر indicates orientation/impact 'upon/against'. تاریک دار repeats the imperative pattern with تاریک ('dark') as predicate and دار ('keep') as the imperative verb: 'keep (the world) dark for the ill‑willed.' The sentence gives contrasting imperatives: favor the wise, oppress or obscure the world for malicious people.
segments.s443-g33: همین is a demonstrative meaning “this (very)”. بودش is an archaic past copula/possessive clitic (بود + ش) meaning “it was” / “he had it” and introduces the result. از رنج و ز گنج are two parallel prepositional phrases (“from hardship and from treasure”), where ز is the poetic variant of از. بهر functions as “for / as (the outcome or recompense)”, linking these phrases to what follows.
segments.s443-g34: بدید is the archaic past tense ‘he saw’ (equivalent to modern دید). از پس together yields a temporal sense “after”. نوش here means a pleasurable drink (wine) and تریاک is opium; زهر is the direct object placed at the end: “he saw / was shown poison after the drink and opium.” Word order places the verb up front in this poetic line.
segments.s443-g35: اگر introduces a conditional/hypothetical. بودن is the infinitive/gerund “to be” (here: existence/being). اینست is the copula “this is,” and شادی is the noun “joy.” چراست is rhetorical here meaning “why is it so?” — the speaker questions the claim that such being is happiness.
segments.s443-g36: شد is the past/result verb “it became / it happened.” از introduces the source/cause (“of/from”), so از مرگ درویش با شاه is a genitive-like phrase “from the death of the dervish with (i.e. alongside) the king.” راست is a predicative adjective “true / right,” so the clause says that, as it turned out, the dervish’s death beside the king proved (or showed) the truth (of the point).
segments.s443-g37: بخور is an imperative “eat / accept” (here idiomatic: accept/undergo). هرچ means “whatever,” and برزی is an archaic verb “falls / comes” (so هرچ برزی = “whatever befalls”). و links the clauses; بد را مکوش is a negative imperative: مکوش (مکو + ش) = “do not attempt / do not seek,” with را marking بد (“evil”) as the object — “do not seek evil.”
segments.s443-g38: به is the preposition “to/for.” مرد خردمند is a noun phrase “a wise man” (مرد = man/person, خردمند = wise). بسپار is the imperative “entrust / give over,” and گوش here (literally “ear”) functions idiomatically as “attention” — so the phrase means “entrust (your) ear to a wise man,” i.e. “listen to the wise.”
segments.s443-g39: گذر is a noun ‘passage’ used with کرد (to make/do) to form the verbal idea ‘passed.’ همراه here indicates accompaniment (“with / together”). The conjunction و links to ما (we) and ماندیم (we remained) — a straightforward past narrative sequence: ‘He passed on with companions and we remained.’
segments.s443-g40: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from,’ and کار گذشته is a compound meaning ‘past affairs / deeds.’ بسی is an adverb meaning ‘much / a great deal,’ and خواندیم is past tense ‘we read / recited’ (here: we recounted or related many past matters). The whole clause: ‘From past deeds we recited much.’
segments.s443-g41: به منزل رسید is a verbal clause with the preposition به placed before منزل (direction/goal) and the past verb رسید (3rd singular) after it: 'reached the station/abode'. آنک introduces a relative clause (archaic form of آن که) and is followed by پوینده بود, where پوینده is a participial adjective/noun 'one who moves / a traveler' and بود is the past copula. Word order is typical classical Persian: goal + verb, then a relative clause describing the subject.
segments.s443-g42: رهی یافت is a compact clause where رهی (archaic spelling meaning 'a way / a path') functions as the object and یافت is the past verb 'found'. آن کس که جوینده بود is a fronted nominal + relative clause ('that person who was a seeker'), with که introducing the relative and بود as the past copula. The clause therefore reads 'found a way the one who was a seeker' → naturalized as 'the seeker found a way.'
segments.s443-g43: نگیرد is a negative present/future 3rd-person form (ن + گیرد) meaning 'will not seize / will not take'. The enclitic ت marks the 2nd-person object 'you'; را is the object marker (classical usage sometimes uses both suffix and را). دست is the agent noun 'hand' (here 'hand' = 'force/hand'), جز introduces the exception 'except', and نیکوی is an adjective used substantively 'the good one'.
segments.s443-g44: گر is the conditional 'if'. از introduces the source/author of the speech, پیر دانا is a nominal phrase 'the old/wise elder' (پیر here = elder/guide), سخن is 'speech/words', and بشنوی is the present-subjunctive/optative 2nd-person form of شنیدن 'you hear' used in the protasis of the conditional: 'if you hear the words of the wise elder.'
segments.s443-g45: کنون 'now' is a temporal adverb. رنج در کار بهمن is a genitival phrase 'suffering/hardship in the affair of Bahman' (در کار = 'in the matter/affair of'); بهمن is a proper name here. بریم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of بردن/بر داشتن meaning 'let us take/let us endure' (classical Persian often uses بریم for a hortatory 'let us ...').
segments.s443-g46: خرد is the direct object 'wisdom'. پیش + دانا پشوتن means 'in the presence of / before the wise Peshotan' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', دانا modifies پشوتن). بریم repeats the 1st-plural optative 'let us bring/let us present'. The whole clause reads 'let us bring wisdom before the wise Peshotan.'
segments.s443-g1: چو (temporal conjunction) + past شد form a subordinate time-clause: 'when ... became'. روزگار تهمتن is the noun phrase 'the days/age of Tahmatan' and به سر is a circumstantial phrase 'to an end' that combines with شد to mean 'came to an end'. Word order places the temporal clause first.
segments.s443-g2: آورم is a first-person singular present (narrative tense used to report/relate) meaning 'I bring/relate'; به پیش is an adverbial 'before/in the presence of' or 'forth' and governs the verb. داستانی دگر is the object 'another tale' (داستانی = tale, دگر = another), placed after the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s443-g3: چو introduces another temporal clause 'when...'. گشتاسپ را uses را to mark the person affected by the change of state. تیره شد (tireh + past شد) = 'grew dark/turned ominous'; روی بخت is a compound 'the face of fortune' (روی = face, بخت = fortune) serving as the theme that darkened for Gushtasp.
segments.s443-g4: بیاورد is a 3rd-person past form 'he brought' / 'he summoned'. جاماسپ را is the definite object marked by را. پیش تخت is a locative phrase 'before the throne' (پیش = in the presence of/before + تخت = throne), placed after the object for emphasis in poetic order.
segments.s443-g5: بدو is an indirect-object phrase 'to him' placed before the reporting verb. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used to introduce reported speech); کز ('که' + ز) introduces the subordinate clause giving the content/reason: 'that/from which (i.e. because of) the deeds of Esfandiyar...'. کار اسفندیار is the noun phrase 'Esfandiyar's deeds/affairs'.
segments.s443-g6: چنان modifies the resulting state 'so/thus'. داغ دل گشتم is a verbal phrase where داغ دل (a burned/wounded heart) is the predicate and گشتم (past of گشتن) means 'I became'—in reported speech the verb is understood in context as 'had become'. The conjunction و links a second nominal predicate سوکوار ('an archer/marksman'), with the verb from the previous clause understood to apply to it (elliptical repetition of 'گشتم').
segments.s443-g7: که introduces an explanatory clause. روزی here (archaic/poetic) means 'once/one day'; نبد is an older negative past form 'was not' (negating the following predicate). زندگانیم ('my living/life') plus خوش ('happy') yields 'there was a day when my life was not happy'—word order shows the negator nبد before the predicate.
segments.s443-g8: دژم بودم is a simple past statement 'I was sullen/gloomy' (بودم = I was). از introduces the cause 'because of/from', and اختر کینه‌کش is an adjectival-nominal phrase 'a vindictive (hate-bearing) star'—a poetic image for hostile fate—giving the reason for his gloom.
segments.s443-g25: همه is an intensifier placed before the noun ('wholly, entirely'). راستی کن is an imperative idiom: راستی (uprightness) + کن (imperative of کردن) = 'be righteous / practise uprightness.' که introduces the following result/purpose clause ('so that'). از راستی uses از as a preposition meaning 'by/from' + راستی repeated for emphasis.
segments.s443-g26: بپیچد is a classical present/optative form with the prefix ب- expressing that something 'may/does turn' (3rd person). سر is the literal 'head' used idiomatically ('one's head/attention turns'). از introduces the source/point 'away from', followed by the two nouns کژی و کاستی (crookedness and deficiency) as the things being turned away from.
segments.s443-g27: سپردم is past first‑person 'I entrusted/handed over.' The enclitic ت marks the indirect object 'to you' (colloquial 2nd‑person suffix). را is the object/definite marker on the whole list تخت و دیهیم و گنج (throne, the rural domain/village, and treasure) as the things entrusted.
segments.s443-g28: ازان پس که is a temporal connector 'after that, since' (ازان + پس + که) introducing the reason/time clause. بردم is past 'I took/bore' (here 'I endured'). بسی functions as an intensifier ('much' / 'greatly') modifying گرم و رنج ('heat and suffering'); گرم is literal 'heat' (metaphorical for hardship) and رنج is 'suffering, hardship.'
segments.s443-g29: بگفت is simple past 'he said.' این points back to the foregoing statement. و شد forms a sequence 'and it happened / and it became'; روزگارش ('his time/days') is the subject of شد. به سر is part of the idiom به سر آمدن/به سر شدن meaning 'to come to an end' ('his time came to an end').
segments.s443-g30: زمان گذشته is 'the past time' or 'former days.' نیامد is the negated past 'did not come.' به بر is an older locative/directional phrasing ('to/for/onto him' or 'back'); here the clause means that the former time did not return to him.
segments.s443-g31: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / once' introducing the action. دخمه 'tomb/vault' is the direct object. کردندش is past plural 'they made/did it' with the enclitic ش marking 'him' as the object. از before شیز و عاج marks the material or composition ('of/from shiz and ivory').
segments.s443-g32: برآویختند is past plural 'they hung' or 'they suspended' (compound verb with prefix بر-). از بر is a classical pairing meaning 'from beside / from upon'; گاه here functions as a locative 'place/spot' linked to تاج ('the crown'), so از بر گاه تاج means roughly 'from the place of the crown.'
segments.s443-g9: پس از من is a prepositional phrase meaning “after me” (پس + از = “after/of”), with کنون (“now”) as an adverbial inserted before the nominal clause. شاه بهمن بود is a past‑copula clause (بود = past of بودن) meaning “Bahman was king” (Persian allows the predicate + بود order; here it reads naturally as “Bahman was king”).
segments.s443-g10: همان modifies رازدارش and means “that very / the same.” رازدارش is رازدار (confidant) plus the clitic ـش (“his”), so “his confidant.” پشوتن is a proper name and بود is the past copula, so the clause states that the confidant was Peshotan.
segments.s443-g11: مپیچید is an imperfect/habitual form (mi‑ prefix in classical style) meaning “(would) turn/bend” or “bow.” سرها (literally “heads”) is the plural subject/affected thing, and ز فرمان اوی uses ز = “from/by” with فرمان (“command”) and اوی (archaic/poetic ‘of him’/‘his’), so the clause means that heads turned/bowed because of his command.
segments.s443-g12: مگیرید is another mi‑imperfect form (here conveying repeated/persistent action) meaning “they would (take/maintain).” دوری ز پیمان اوی = “distance/separation from his covenant”; with ز = “from” and اوی = “his.” The clause therefore says they did not abandon or withdraw from his pledge (the sense is preservative/loyalty).
segments.s443-g13: یکایک is an adverb “one by one / each.” بویدش is an archaic/poetic verbal form (literally derived from بویدن “to smell”), here used figuratively to mean that the envoys made themselves known or appeared as his representatives; the final ـش is the object/associative clitic referring back to “him.” نماینده راه = “representatives/agents of the road,” i.e. messengers travelling on the road.
segments.s443-g14: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause (“who/for he is”). اویست here functions as a proper name or identifying label (Owais) in apposition. زیبای تخت و کلاه is a nominal phrase “the beauty/beautiful one of the throne and the crown” (زیبای = adjectival/nominalized form meaning ‘the beautiful/beauty of’), so the clause identifies him as Owais, the handsome one of throne and crown.
segments.s443-g15: بدو is the clitic combination of به + او = “to him/unto him.” داد is the past of دادن (“gave”), and پس is an adverb “then/after.” گنجها را کلید places the object marker را after گنجها (“the treasures”), with کلید (“key”) as the thing given — i.e. “the key of the treasures.” Word order is slightly inverted but standard in poetry.
segments.s443-g16: یکی is an indefinite “one / a certain / suddenly.” باد سرد literally “a cold wind” here works as a metaphor for an internal chill or pang. از جگر = “from the liver” (classical Persian idiom for the seat of feeling/heart). برکشید is a past verb “rose up / drew up” (literally ‘lifted’); together the line means a cold pang rose from his heart.

sentences.s443-s9: He said to him, "My business is finished; even the water of my tarak has passed beyond."
sentences.s443-s10: I sat on the throne for one hundred and twenty years; I have not seen anyone in the world his equal.
sentences.s443-s11: You now strive (keep striving) and be just; when you bring justice, be free from sorrow.
sentences.s443-s12: Keep the wise happy and near; keep the world dark for the ill‑willed.
sentences.s443-s17: This, as the reward of his toil and of his treasure, he found — after drink and opium — poison.
sentences.s443-s18: If this existence is called joy, then why? The dervish’s death beside the king turned out to be true (i.e. showed the truth).
sentences.s443-s19: Accept whatever befalls you, and do not pursue evil; give your ear to a wise man.
sentences.s443-s20: He passed on with his companions, and we remained; from past affairs we recited much.
sentences.s443-s21: He who travelled reached a stopping-place; the one who sought found a way.
sentences.s443-s22: No hand will seize you except a good one, if you hear the words of a wise elder.
sentences.s443-s23: Now let us endure hardship in Bahman's affair; let us take wisdom before the wise Peshotan.
sentences.s443-s1: When the days of Tahmatan came to an end, I bring forward another tale.
sentences.s443-s2: When the face of fortune darkened for Gushtasp, he summoned Jamasp before the throne.
sentences.s443-s3: He said to him that, because of Esfandiyar's deeds, he had become so heart-sore and a marksman.
sentences.s443-s4: for there came a day when my life was not happy; I was sullen because of a vindictive star.
sentences.s443-s13: Keep wholly to righteousness, so that by righteousness the head may turn away from crookedness and lack.
sentences.s443-s14: I entrusted to you the throne, the rural domain, and the treasure, after I had borne much heat and hardship.
sentences.s443-s15: He said this, and his days came to an end; the past time did not return to him.
sentences.s443-s16: They made for him a tomb of shiz and ivory, and they hung him from the place of the crown.
sentences.s443-s5: After me, Bahman was now king, and that same confidant was Peshotan.
sentences.s443-s6: Heads bowed at his command; they did not withdraw from his covenant.
sentences.s443-s7: One by one his messengers came on the road; he was Owais, the handsome one of throne and crown.
sentences.s443-s8: Then he gave him the key to the treasures; a cold pang rose from his heart.
